planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost for spring planting, or in midsummer for a fall crop (most reliable season in the South); Cheddar is a yellow-orange cauliflower with dramatically higher beta-carotene content than white varieties — the orange color intensifies when cooked rather than fading, and the flavor is slightly sweeter and nuttier than standard white cauliflower, making it visually distinctive and nutritionally superior.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; set out transplants in rich, moist, well-drained soil 18–24 inches apart; keep consistently moist; in warm climates (zones 7–10) plant for fall harvest from summer transplants for best head development; harvest when heads are tight and before the curds begin to separate. Cheddar is a yellow-orange cauliflower that gets its remarkable color from high concentrations of beta-carotene — the same orange pigment found in carrots, sweet potatoes, and winter squash — resulting in orange cauliflower heads that retain and even intensify their color when cooked (unlike red cabbage, which loses color to cooking liquid). The orange coloring signals significantly higher nutritional value than white cauliflower: approximately 25 times more beta-carotene per serving, providing provitamin A activity that white cauliflower lacks. The flavor is slightly sweeter and nuttier than standard white varieties, and the heads hold their color well through roasting, steaming, and cauliflower 'steaks' — making Cheddar cauliflower one of the most visually dramatic and nutritionally enriched brassicas for the home garden. The AAS award-winning orange/yellow Cheddar type was developed by Cornell University researchers and initially commercialized by Bejo Seeds; it performs best as a fall crop in warm climates where summer transplants mature into cooler fall temperatures that favor head development.
sources: Cornell University Department of Plant Breeding

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost for spring planting or in midsummer for fall crop; Graffiti is a vivid purple cauliflower whose dramatic color comes from anthocyanins — the same antioxidant pigments in red cabbage and blueberries — and while the color fades when cooked (unlike Cheddar's beta-carotene), the raw purple makes it spectacularly visual in crudités platters, salads, and vegetable displays.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; transplant into rich, moist, fertile soil with consistent watering; fall crops typically produce the most vivid purple color as cool temperatures intensify the anthocyanin pigmentation; harvest when heads are tight. Graffiti is a vivid purple cauliflower developed by Syngenta Seeds, with intensely purple heads caused by very high anthocyanin concentrations — the same pigments responsible for the colors of red cabbage, blueberries, blackberries, and purple sweet potatoes, and associated with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory health properties. Unlike the orange beta-carotene in Cheddar cauliflower, the purple anthocyanins in Graffiti are water-soluble and somewhat heat-sensitive: the vivid purple color turns gray-blue or fades when the vegetable is boiled or steamed, though the color remains more stable with roasting or stir-frying. For maximum visual impact, Graffiti is best used raw — in crudités platters, purple cauliflower hummus, raw salads, and grain bowls where the intense purple provides a visual anchor. The flavor is typically cauliflower — slightly nutty, mild, sweet — with no unusual taste despite the dramatic color. Cool temperatures intensify the purple color, making fall-grown Graffiti more vividly colored than spring crops. An excellent conversation piece and visual anchor for vegetable gardens.

common_name: Di Sicilia Viola Cauliflower (Sicilian Purple Heritage Cauliflower)
scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. botrytis 'Di Sicilia Viola'

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before fall transplanting (best as a fall crop in most climates); Di Sicilia Viola is a Sicilian heirloom purple cauliflower with deep traditional roots in the vegetable gardens of Sicily, where purple cauliflower has been grown for centuries — it matures later than modern purple hybrids but has exceptional flavor and cultural significance as a true heirloom variety.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before planned fall transplanting date; set out in rich, fertile, moist soil; Di Sicilia Viola matures later than modern hybrids (85–100 days) but produces heads of exceptional flavor — considered by Italian cooks to be superior to any hybrid purple cauliflower. Di Sicilia Viola (Sicilian Violet) is a traditional heirloom purple cauliflower from Sicily, where purple and violet cauliflower varieties have been grown for centuries as an important winter vegetable in Sicilian cuisine. The Sicilian purple types are the oldest cultivated purple cauliflowers in the world and are quite distinct from modern purple hybrids like Graffiti — they mature later (85–100 days versus 70–80 for hybrids), produce looser, less uniformly round heads, and have a distinctly different flavor: richer, more complex, and sweeter than modern varieties, with a particular quality when braised or roasted that traditional Sicilian cooks consider superior to any modern hybrid. In Sicily, Di Sicilia Viola is used in pasta con i cavolfiori alla palermitana (pasta with cauliflower, olives, pine nuts, raisins, and saffron — one of the signature dishes of Palermo), roasted with olive oil and capers, and in arancini (Sicilian rice balls). The longer maturity makes it best suited as a fall crop in zones 5–9, where it matures into cool fall weather that intensifies the purple color and sweetens the flavor.

common_name: Scarlet Ohno Revival Turnip (Red-Leafed Dual-Purpose Japanese Turnip)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer or fall for fall harvest; Scarlet Ohno Revival is a visually stunning Japanese turnip with dramatic red-scarlet to magenta skin and flesh — one of the most beautiful root vegetables available — with mild, sweet turnip flavor at the small 2–3 inch stage; both the root and the brilliant red-tinged leaves are edible, making it a dual-purpose vegetable.

directions: Sow directly 1/4 inch deep in cool weather; thin to 4–6 inches apart for best root development; harvest at 2–3 inches diameter for the sweetest, mildest eating quality; the red coloration intensifies in cool weather; the leaves can be harvested as greens throughout the season. Scarlet Ohno Revival (sometimes marketed as 'Scarlet Queen Turnip') is a Japanese turnip variety with striking visual characteristics: the round roots have vivid scarlet to magenta-red skin with pink-red flesh throughout — much more deeply and uniformly colored than the more common 'Tokyo' types — and the broad basal leaves have dramatic red-tinged stems and midribs that make the plant ornamental in the garden as well as functional. The turnip was selected from an older Japanese variety ('Ohno Scarlet') by seed savers and represents a revival of an older type that had become rare. At harvest size (2–3 inches), the roots have a mild, slightly sweet turnip flavor quite different from the sharp, peppery bite of older turnip types — the Japanese breeding tradition favors mild, salad-turnip types that can be eaten raw, and Scarlet Ohno follows this tradition. The roots are excellent sliced thin into salads (the red color is dramatic against green lettuce), quick-pickled in the Japanese style (the red pigment turns the pickling brine a beautiful deep pink), roasted with the skin on (which deepens the color), or used in any turnip recipe where visual impact is valued.

common_name: Tokyo Market Turnip (Japanese Fast-Growing Salad Turnip)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer for fall harvest or in early spring; Tokyo Market is a very fast-maturing Japanese salad turnip (30–40 days) producing small, round, smooth, pure white roots with very mild, sweet flavor — harvested at golf-ball to tennis-ball size for the finest eating quality; similar in type to Hakurei but a slightly larger, more market-focused variety.

directions: Sow directly 1/4 inch deep in cool weather; thin to 3–4 inches apart; harvest at 2–3 inch diameter for finest quality (overripe roots become pithy and strongly flavored); both roots and tender greens are edible. Tokyo Market is a Japanese-type salad turnip closely related to (but distinct from) Hakurei — both belong to the mild, sweet, fast-maturing Japanese turnip tradition that is quite different from the traditional European turnip types grown for cooking. Tokyo Market matures in just 30–40 days from direct seeding, producing smooth, round, pure white roots with a mild, slightly sweet, and almost fruity flavor that is excellent raw — sliced thin into salads, served with dipping sauces, or eaten out of hand like an apple in the garden. Unlike European cooking turnips (which are generally too sharp and starchy to eat raw), Japanese salad turnip types like Tokyo Market and Hakurei have been bred for low pungency and high sugar content, making them the most accessible and enjoyable turnips for most modern palates. The tender, mild young greens are equally edible — excellent in salads, stir-fries, and anywhere turnip greens are used with a preference for milder flavor. Tokyo Market is preferred in farmers market growing for its consistent, uniform appearance and rapid turnover; for home gardeners it provides the fastest-maturing turnip option for succession planting every 3–4 weeks.
